figures.caption: Figure 1. Accuracy-efficiency trade-off on the Burgers benchmark. Our Spatio-Temporal MeanFlow achieves an optimal Pareto frontier (bottom-left), delivering the lowest relative L2 error with highly competitive inference speeds across all baselines.

Table 2. Performance and computational cost on the Burgers benchmark. We report the relative L2 error (×10−2), inference time (milliseconds per sample), and GFLOPs per sample. Lower is better. The best results are highlighted with bold text and a gray background.
Figure 5. Visual comparison of predicted fields and error maps on ns dataset.
Figure 6. Training trajectories for all loss components, including the total loss, the data loss, the temporal MeanFlow loss, and the spatial MeanFlow loss.

Figure 7. Additional coefficient analysis on Burgers’ equation. Left: spatial MeanFlow coefficient sweep with fixed temporal MeanFlow coefficient λT-MF = 0.01. Right: temporal MeanFlow coefficient sweep with fixed spatial MeanFlow coefficient λS-MF = 0.01. The reported metric is global relative ℓ2 error at the base resolution s = 128; lower is better.

Table 3. Ablation study on Burgers evaluating the effects of temporal and spatial MeanFlow losses, resolution generalization and out-of-distribution initial condition generalization. All models are trained at the base resolution s = 128 and evaluated on test sets generated with the same random seed for each setting. We report global relative L2 error; lower is better.

Figure 2. Overview of our framework. (A) Spatio-Temporal Path Dynamics, formulating PDE solutions via straight paths. (B) Space-Time Decoupling, constraining the surface in space or time direction only, designed to mitigate scale mismatches within the Hessian matrix.
Figure 4. Qualitative visualization on the NS dataset. We compare ground-truth vorticity fields with predictions from our method at three time steps for two test samples.
